H-OS-L1.name: Pebble
H-OS-L1.sphere: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80, confidence=strong) is the largest-sample protocol in the corpus and returns a moderate positive signal with notable spread. The distribution shows a concentration at score 6 (19 respondents) with reasonable tails in both directions, suggesting a population split between strong responders and those experiencing minimal change. Qualitative data (n=27) shows a diverse listener mix and Driving/commuting as a top activity context (5/27) — relevant for an over-stimulation protocol, where environmental noise may interfere with entrainment.
H-OS-L1.sphere_plain: Pebble has the most responses of any protocol in the library, which gives us real confidence in what we're seeing. Results are positive on average but vary quite a bit between individuals — some people feel a strong shift, others less so. A number of listeners report using it while driving, which is an interesting real-world use case for a protocol designed to help with overstimulation.

H-OS-L2.name: Rainfall
H-OS-L2.sphere: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85, confidence=strong) shows improvement over L1 in mean score, consistent with expected level progression. The settle_time data clusters at 10 minutes (10/20 qualitative respondents), suggesting Rainfall's entrainment arc is well-matched to listener expectation at this depth. Rhythm perception is evenly split between 'Very settling/calming' and 'Balanced/comfortable', both positive categories, indicating no friction with the DESCENT structure.
H-OS-L2.sphere_plain: Rainfall shows improvement over Pebble, with most qualitative respondents settling around the 10-minute mark — a sign the pacing feels right. People describe the rhythm warmly, and there's no notable friction. A solid, reliable second step in the overstimulation series.

H-OS-L3.name: Glacier
H-OS-L3.sphere: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78, confidence=meaningful) reaches the highest mean in the OS subcategory and shows strong rhythm reception — a perfect split between 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Very settling/calming' (10 each, n=23 qualitative). Activity context is dominated by 'Trying to sleep' (8/23), which is off-label for an over-stimulation protocol but suggests listeners are self-routing Glacier as a sleep aid — a potential cross-series routing signal worth investigating.
H-OS-L3.sphere_plain: Glacier is the strongest overstimulation protocol and receives consistently warm feedback on both music and rhythm. Interestingly, a lot of people seem to be using it to help them sleep — even though it's technically designed for overstimulation. That overlap is worth exploring: Glacier may be quietly doing double duty as a sleep aid for some listeners.

H-AX-L1.name: Meadow
H-AX-L1.sphere: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89, confidence=strong) is the highest-performing Anxiety & Stress protocol and among the strongest in the corpus, though its std of 1.89 indicates meaningful spread across the population. The distribution is right-weighted with peaks at scores 6–9, consistent with broad but not universal benefit. Qualitative data (n=23) shows a mixed listener population with notable caregiver and clinician presence (10/23 non-self reporters), change_rating dominated by Moderate and Noticeable improvement, and rhythm perceived as Balanced/comfortable by a strong majority — suggesting the protocol's entrainment rate is well-calibrated for its DESCENT function.
H-AX-L1.sphere_plain: Meadow is one of the better-performing protocols in the whole library — most people who use it report feeling noticeably calmer afterward, and the results are consistent enough to take seriously with 65 responses. There's still quite a range in how much it helps different people, which is normal. Caregivers and therapists are using it alongside individuals, and most describe it as balanced and comfortable to listen to — not too much, not too little.

H-AX-L2.name: Cottage
H-AX-L2.sphere: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.90, std=1.63, confidence=strong) shows tighter variance than L1 while maintaining comparable central tendency, which may reflect a more homogeneous responding population at L2. The distribution is reasonably symmetric around 7–8. Qualitative data (n=20) flags a bimodal settle_time split between 5 and 20 minutes — potentially reflecting two listener subgroups: those who entrain quickly and those requiring the full session. Driving/commuting appearing as a notable activity context (4/20) warrants attention given DESCENT protocol mechanics.
H-AX-L2.sphere_plain: Cottage continues the calming arc from Meadow and holds up well as a second step. Most people report real improvement, and the tighter spread of results suggests it's reaching a fairly consistent group of listeners. One thing worth noting: a good number of people seem to be listening while driving, which is a bit unexpected for a protocol designed to help you slow down — something to keep an eye on.

H-AX-L3.name: Valley
H-AX-L3.sphere: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81, confidence=meaningful) performs strongly at L3 and maintains the subcategory's broadly positive signal. The qualitative settle_time data is notable: 'Near the end — the entire session' appears as a secondary mode (4/20), suggesting some listeners require the full arc of a longer protocol to regulate — consistent with the WING RATIO architecture producing more gradual entrainment at L3. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling/calming' most frequently, the strongest positive rhythm rating in the AX subcategory.
H-AX-L3.sphere_plain: Valley, the deepest anxiety protocol, performs just as well as the earlier levels — which is reassuring. Some people need almost the whole session before they start to feel the shift, which makes sense for a longer, more gradual experience. Listeners describe the rhythm as especially calming at this level, more so than in Meadow or Cottage, suggesting the deeper architecture is doing its job.

H-CR-L1.name: Anchor
H-CR-L1.sphere: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) performs above structural expectation for a crisis-entry protocol, where depressed baseline states typically compress outcome scores. pct_pos at 52% is low in absolute terms but appropriate given the population. Qualitative data (n=24) is notably positive: change_rating is unusually strong with 8/24 reporting Significant improvement — the highest in the crisis subcategory — and music reception is warm. The 'Other' activity category dominating (6/24) suggests unstructured, spontaneous use in distress moments, which is exactly the intended deployment context.
H-CR-L1.sphere_plain: Anchor is designed for moments of real distress, so the results need to be read carefully — people arrive in difficult states, and even getting to a 6 out of 10 from there is meaningful. What stands out in the qualitative responses is that a surprising number of people report significant improvement, more than you'd expect for a crisis protocol. It's clearly being used spontaneously in hard moments, which is exactly what it's there for.

H-CR-L2.name: Harbor
H-CR-L2.sphere: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67, confidence=meaningful) is the weakest performer in the crisis subcategory and notable for being the only crisis protocol where music_opinion mode is 'Neutral/so-so' rather than positive. Settle_time shows a secondary mode at 20 minutes, suggesting entrainment may be too gradual for listeners in acute distress who have already completed L1. Confidence is meaningful but warrants structural review before the sample grows further.
H-CR-L2.sphere_plain: Harbor is the softest performer in the crisis group, and the qualitative responses add some texture to why: the music gets a more lukewarm reception than Anchor or Horizon, and some people take a long time to settle — up to 20 minutes. For people in acute distress, a slow start may not be ideal. This one warrants a closer look at whether the opening section is doing enough to create early steadiness.

H-CR-L3.name: Horizon
H-CR-L3.sphere: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85, confidence=meaningful) recovers slightly from L2, which is an unexpected non-monotonic pattern within the crisis arc. Qualitative settle_time is striking: 'Immediately/within the first minute or two' is the modal response (7/19), suggesting Horizon may be working for listeners who are primed from earlier protocols and respond rapidly to a familiar sonic environment. However, 4/19 music_opinion responses are 'Not liked' — the highest negative music rating in the HEALING series — warranting a review of the L3 sonic palette.
H-CR-L3.sphere_plain: Horizon, the third crisis protocol, recovers a little from Harbor's dip, which is an interesting pattern. Many people report settling very quickly — sometimes within the first couple of minutes — possibly because they've already been through the earlier protocols and the sound feels familiar. A small but notable group didn't enjoy the music, which is worth paying attention to given that the other crisis protocols receive warmer responses.

H-SL-L1.name: Lantern
H-SL-L1.sphere: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89, confidence=strong) performs well with a broad distribution spanning scores 5–9. Qualitative data (n=15) is unexpectedly light relative to the outcome n, limiting contextual inference, but shows warm music reception (10/15 Liked, 3/15 Loved) and fast settle_time for many respondents. The Therapy session activity appearing (3/15) alongside Studying is an unusual context for a sleep-oriented protocol — may reflect clinicians using it for general relaxation induction.
H-SL-L1.sphere_plain: Lantern performs well as a sleep entry point, with a mostly positive response and listeners describing it as easy and pleasant. The sample for the qualitative feedback is smaller than expected relative to how many people completed the quick survey, which limits what we can say about how people are actually experiencing it. What we do have suggests it's being used in therapy contexts as well as at home.

H-SL-L2.name: Hammock
H-SL-L2.sphere: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46, confidence=meaningful) has the tightest standard deviation in the HEALING series, suggesting a more homogeneous response profile than its siblings — a possible indicator that Hammock is reaching a well-matched population. However, qualitative data flags concerns: listener_type is dominated by clients/patients and support-persons rather than self-listeners (11/15 non-self), music_opinion mode is 'Neutral/so-so', and rhythm receives a 'Too busy' rating from 3/15 respondents — the only appearance of that category in the sleep subcategory. This warrants investigation given the low-variability outcome score may be masking population-specific mismatches.
H-SL-L2.sphere_plain: Hammock has the most consistent results in the sleep series — people's responses cluster more tightly around a similar score than in the other sleep protocols, which suggests it's reaching a reliable group. However, the qualitative picture is more mixed: a few people find it a bit too rhythmically busy for a sleep protocol, and the music gets a cooler reception than Lantern. Worth investigating whether the pacing needs to ease off a little.

H-SL-L3.name: Midnight
H-SL-L3.sphere: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57, confidence=meaningful) maintains strong performance at L3 with consistent change_rating distribution in qualitative data. Notably, 7/22 qualitative respondents report 'Slightly busy' rhythm — an elevated rate for a sleep-descent protocol at L3, where maximum settling would be expected. This may reflect the phi-sigmoid architecture's longer wing ratios producing perceptible rhythmic movement even at low tempos, and warrants a review of Midnight's L3 complexity level.
H-SL-L3.sphere_plain: Midnight performs strongly as the deepest sleep protocol, but a notable proportion of qualitative respondents describe the rhythm as 'slightly busy' — which feels counterintuitive for something designed to help you drift off. This may be an inherent feature of how longer protocols are structured, but it's worth exploring whether the final phase could be quieter and simpler.

T-FC-L1.name: Compass
T-FC-L1.sphere: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.40, std=1.77, confidence=meaningful) shows the lowest mean in the Focus & Clarity subcategory despite having the largest sample. The qualitative data (n=23) is revealing: rhythm_opinion mode is 'Slightly busy' (9/23) — the strongest busy signal in the THRIVING series — while music is warmly received (15/23 Liked). This dissociation between music appreciation and rhythm perception suggests the HOLD architecture may be introducing too much textural movement for a focus-entry protocol, where listeners need clean cognitive scaffolding rather than rhythmic engagement.
T-FC-L1.sphere_plain: Compass, the entry point for Focus & Clarity, is actually the lowest-scoring protocol in its subcategory — and the qualitative data hints at why. A lot of people describe the rhythm as 'slightly busy', which for a protocol meant to help you concentrate is a meaningful friction point. The music itself is liked, so this seems to be about the texture and pace rather than the sound quality.

T-FC-L2.name: Waypoint
T-FC-L2.sphere: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51, confidence=meaningful) is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity subcategory and shows a well-distributed outcome score with a peak at score 8. The settle_time mode at 'About 10 minutes' (9/17 qualitative) is consistent with the longer entrainment curve expected at L2. The 'Slightly busy' rhythm rating from 5/17 respondents is lower than L1's rate but still present, suggesting a thread of complexity that persists across the FC subcategory.
T-FC-L2.sphere_plain: Waypoint is the best-performing Focus & Clarity protocol and shows the kind of improvement over L1 you'd hope to see. People settle around the 10-minute mark, which feels right for this kind of deeper focus work. A thread of 'slightly busy' rhythm perception persists, but it's less prominent than at L1 — suggesting the architecture is moving in the right direction.

T-FC-L3.name: Summit
T-FC-L3.sphere: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38, confidence=meaningful) is the primary anomaly in the THRIVING series: a clear underperformance relative to L2 in a subcategory where the protocol should be deepening benefit. Qualitative change_rating is paradoxically the strongest in the FC subcategory (11/26 report Significant improvement), creating a disconnect between 1-second outcome scores and 1-minute change perception that may reflect different response windows or population self-selection at L3. Settle_time at 10 minutes is the dominant mode (14/26), and rhythm 'Slightly busy' persists (6/26), consistent with a protocol that demands more from the listener than it returns.
T-FC-L3.sphere_plain: Summit is the most surprising result in the THRIVING series: it actually scores lower than Waypoint, when you'd expect the deepest protocol to go further. Paradoxically, people's own descriptions of their improvement are the most positive in the Focus & Clarity group — they feel like it helped — but the immediate outcome scores don't reflect that. This disconnect is worth investigating. The rhythm is still perceived as busy, and people take longer to settle at this level than might be ideal.

T-CF-L1.name: Ember
T-CF-L1.sphere: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63, confidence=meaningful) returns a moderate positive signal for a Creative Flow entry protocol. The qualitative settle_time is bimodal — 'Immediately' and 'About 10 minutes' are tied at 6 respondents each — which may reflect two listener populations: those using Ember for flow-state maintenance versus those arriving in a less-optimal state and requiring longer entrainment. Change_rating distribution is spread across Slight, Moderate, and Significant improvement, suggesting Ember is providing differentiated benefit across listener states.
T-CF-L1.sphere_plain: Ember gets creative flow started with moderate success — people report improvement, though often in the slight-to-moderate range rather than dramatic. There seem to be two different kinds of listeners using it: some settle almost immediately, others take closer to ten minutes. That split might reflect people using it in very different states when they press play.

T-CF-L2.name: Current
T-CF-L2.sphere: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28, confidence=meaningful) shows the tightest variance in the Creative Flow subcategory and the strongest music reception — mode 'Loved it' tied with 'Liked it' (6 each, n=15 qualitative). The narrow std suggests Current is reaching a consistent subpopulation, possibly committed creative practitioners who self-select into L2. The relatively modest mean given the strong music appreciation warrants investigation: high aesthetic response without proportionally high outcome score may suggest the protocol is engaging but not fully optimising the intended HOLD function.
T-CF-L2.sphere_plain: Current is notable for how much people enjoy the music — it's one of the most loved-sounding protocols in the library. The results are consistent and positive, though the mean score doesn't quite match the enthusiasm for the sound. People seem to really enjoy the listening experience, but the functional flow benefit may not be maximised yet — an interesting design question for the future.

T-CF-L3.name: Aurora
T-CF-L3.sphere: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15, confidence=developing) is the highest-mean Creative Flow protocol and has the tightest variance in the subcategory, though n=18 limits confidence. The qualitative data (n=25 — notably larger than outcome n, suggesting response timing asymmetry) shows strong representation from caregivers and clinicians (13/25 non-self), which may be inflating or deflating the 1-minute responses relative to actual listeners. Rhythm is overwhelmingly 'Balanced/comfortable' (14/25), consistent with a well-calibrated HOLD architecture at depth.
T-CF-L3.sphere_plain: Aurora has the fewest responses of the Creative Flow protocols, so we're holding these findings lightly. What we do see is encouraging — the scores are the highest in the series and the results are quite consistent. Rhythm is described as balanced and comfortable by most. It's worth building the sample before drawing firm conclusions.

T-MB-L1.name: Stone
T-MB-L1.sphere: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31, confidence=meaningful) is the strongest performer in THRIVING and ties with T-CF-L3 for the highest THRIVING mean. Qualitative data shows heavy therapy session use (8/23) and a bimodal settle_time between 'About 20 minutes' and 'About 5 minutes' — an unusually wide spread suggesting Stone is used both as a session anchor and a quick-reset tool. Music reception is mixed but neutral-to-positive, which for a HOLD protocol is functionally appropriate.
T-MB-L1.sphere_plain: Stone is the standout in the THRIVING series — the highest-scoring Maintenance & Balance protocol and one of the top performers overall. It's being used heavily in therapy settings, which may partly explain the strong results. People settle across a wide range of times, suggesting it's flexible enough to work in different contexts and session lengths.

T-MB-L2.name: Axis
T-MB-L2.sphere: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) has the highest standard deviation in the THRIVING series, indicating wider population variance than its siblings. Qualitative rhythm data shows a perfect split between 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Slightly busy' (7 each, n=16), suggesting the protocol sits at a perceptual boundary for complexity tolerance. The client/patient and self-listener split is nearly equal (7:7), which may be contributing to the variance.
T-MB-L2.sphere_plain: Axis shows more variability in outcomes than its siblings — people's experiences range more widely, which may reflect a more diverse audience at this level. The rhythm sits right at the edge of comfortable for some listeners, with a noticeable group finding it slightly busy. Results are still positive overall, but this is one to watch as more data comes in.

T-MB-L3.name: Orbit
T-MB-L3.sphere: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64, confidence=developing) has a structurally unusual score distribution — a dense cluster at score 7 (9 respondents) with sparse values elsewhere and an isolated score at 10, producing a pattern that warrants monitoring for bimodality as n grows. The qualitative 'Did NOT settle' rate of 4/19 is the most concerning signal in the THRIVING series; for a L3 HOLD protocol aimed at balance maintenance, non-settlement may indicate a population routing issue rather than a protocol flaw.
T-MB-L3.sphere_plain: Orbit is the deepest Maintenance & Balance protocol and the data is still early. What stands out is that 4 out of 19 qualitative respondents report they didn't settle at all — that's a higher-than-expected rate for a protocol at this level. It may mean some people are arriving here who aren't quite ready for L3, or it could be a routing issue. Worth investigating before the sample grows further.

X-ME-L1.name: Breeze
X-ME-L1.sphere: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) is the entry point for the Mood & Energy ASCENT subcategory with a moderate positive signal and relatively flat distribution between scores 4–8. Qualitative data shows strong caregiver and clinician presence (11/22 non-self) and settle_time skewing late (modal: About 20 minutes, 6/22). 'Loved it' music_opinion (10/22) is strong, but 'Neutral' also appears prominently (6/22), with rhythm predominantly Balanced — suggesting the ASCENT arc is functioning smoothly but may need a stronger energising signal in the early phase given the long settle times.
X-ME-L1.sphere_plain: Breeze opens the Mood & Energy series with a modest positive signal. People take a while to settle — often around 20 minutes — which may mean the early section of the protocol could do more to signal the shift that's coming. The music is warmly received by many, and caregivers and clinicians are a significant part of the audience, suggesting it's being used in supported contexts.

X-ME-L2.name: Sunrise
X-ME-L2.sphere: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64, confidence=meaningful) improves over L1 in mean and pct_pos (60% vs 43%), which is the expected level progression for an ASCENT subcategory. Qualitative change_rating is strongly weighted toward Significant improvement (7/15), the most positive distribution in the Mood & Energy subcategory. Settle_time is bimodal — 'About 20 minutes' and 'Immediately' tied — which may reflect a similar two-population dynamic as seen in other TRANSFORMING protocols.
X-ME-L2.sphere_plain: Sunrise is the strongest performer in the Mood & Energy series and shows a meaningful step up from Breeze. The qualitative descriptions of improvement are notably positive — more people report significant improvement here than at any other level in the subcategory. The bimodal settle time (some people feeling it immediately, others taking much longer) suggests a mixed audience that responds to the protocol in quite different ways.

X-ME-L3.name: Updraft
X-ME-L3.sphere: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74, confidence=developing) underperforms relative to L2, creating a non-monotonic progression in the Mood & Energy subcategory (6.29 → 6.48 → 6.28). Qualitative change_rating shows 5/25 respondents reporting 'No effect' — the highest no-effect rate in the TRANSFORMING series — alongside 6 reporting Significant improvement, suggesting a polarised response. Activity context dominated by Relaxing and Doing chores (10/25 combined) may indicate Updraft is not being used in optimal conditions for an ascending-arc protocol.
X-ME-L3.sphere_plain: Updraft, the deepest Mood & Energy protocol, dips back below Sunrise — a pattern worth watching. A meaningful number of people report no effect at all, which is the highest no-effect rate in the TRANSFORMING series. At the same time, others report significant improvement, suggesting the protocol may be working well for a specific subset while missing another. People often seem to be using it in low-demand contexts like relaxing or doing chores, which may not be the ideal environment for an ascending-arc protocol meant to lift mood and energy.

X-MD-L1.name: Footpath
X-MD-L1.sphere: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) is the entry point for TRANSFORMING's Motivation & Drive ASCENT subcategory and shows a broad, relatively flat distribution across scores 4–9. Qualitative settle_time is fast (modal response: About 5 minutes, with Immediately as secondary), which is consistent with an ASCENT protocol that begins energising listeners relatively quickly. Music reception is positive-to-neutral and rhythm is warm, suggesting the sonic design is not creating friction even if the motivational outcome is moderate.
X-MD-L1.sphere_plain: Footpath, the entry into Motivation & Drive territory, provides moderate uplift and people settle relatively quickly — which is a good sign for an energising protocol. Results are spread across a wide range, reflecting the real diversity of people who arrive needing a motivational boost. The sonic experience itself lands positively, which gives a good foundation to build on.

X-MD-L2.name: Trail
X-MD-L2.sphere: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.00, std=1.57, confidence=developing) returns the lowest pct_pos (36%) in the Motivation & Drive subcategory and one of the lowest in the corpus. Qualitative data shows 'Loved it' as music_opinion mode (9/21) — a striking dissociation between aesthetic appreciation and functional outcome. Rhythm is perceived as 'Slightly busy' by 6/21 respondents, which for an ASCENT protocol may indicate the drive cues are activating arousal without productive forward-momentum structure. The settle_time skewing late (20 minutes modal) suggests Trail's build phase may peak too slowly for a mid-level motivational protocol.
X-MD-L2.sphere_plain: Trail is one of the most puzzling findings in the dataset. People love the music — it receives some of the warmest music responses in the TRANSFORMING series — but the functional outcomes are among the lowest in the corpus. Something about the listening experience is genuinely enjoyable but may not be translating into the energy and motivation lift it's designed to provide. The rhythm being described as slightly busy is one possible clue: drive cues that activate without directing may create arousal without momentum.

X-MD-L3.name: Switchback
X-MD-L3.sphere: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, confidence=developing) shows the highest mean in the Motivation & Drive subcategory despite the highest standard deviation — a pattern consistent with a protocol that works very well for some listeners and not at all for others. Qualitative change_rating is predominantly Moderate improvement (8/17) with fast settle_time (modal: About 5 minutes), and the activity context is dominated by 'Trying to sleep' (6/17) — an off-label use for an ASCENT protocol that may be artificially depressing outcome scores if listeners are using Switchback as a wind-down tool.
X-MD-L3.sphere_plain: Switchback has the widest spread of results in the Motivation & Drive protocols — some people respond really well, others much less so. Interestingly, a lot of people seem to be using it to try to sleep, even though it's an energising protocol. If that's happening regularly, those listeners are likely to score it lower simply because it's not doing what they need — which could be pulling the overall numbers down.

X-MC-L1.name: Cavern
X-MC-L1.sphere: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56, confidence=meaningful) is the strongest-performing TRANSFORMING protocol in this subcategory and shows the warmest music reception in the series — 'Loved it' and 'Liked it' tied at 9 each (n=25 qualitative). Rhythm is predominantly positive (Balanced/comfortable and Very settling/calming totalling 17/25). Despite 2/25 qualitative respondents reporting 'It actually made things worse', the aggregate signal is constructive. The worse-experience responses warrant a follow-up question addition to understand whether these reflect pre-existing state severity or protocol mismatch.
X-MC-L1.sphere_plain: Cavern, the entry point for TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity protocols, is one of the most warmly received in the whole series — people genuinely seem to love the music and describe the rhythm as calming. Results are positive, though a small number of people reported it made things worse rather than better, which is worth tracking carefully as something to understand rather than dismiss.

X-MC-L2.name: Starlight
X-MC-L2.sphere: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37, confidence=developing) shows a strongly self-listener-dominated population in qualitative data (16/20 reporting for themselves), which is distinctive in the TRANSFORMING series where caregiver/clinician use is more common. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling/calming' by 12/20 respondents — the strongest calming rhythm signal in the entire TRANSFORMING series. Settle_time is bimodal (immediate vs. 10 minutes), potentially reflecting two listener entry states.
X-MC-L2.sphere_plain: Starlight is striking for two reasons: it's used almost entirely by people listening for themselves (rather than caregivers or clinicians), and the rhythm is described as very settling by most — the most calming rhythm response in the whole TRANSFORMING series. Results are positive and consistent. The self-listener focus suggests this protocol may be attracting people who are doing serious, self-directed work on their mental state.

X-MC-L3.name: Cosmos
X-MC-L3.sphere: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.60, std=2.32, confidence=developing) is the highest-variance, lowest-mean protocol in the TRANSFORMING series and the clearest anomaly in the corpus. The wide std at minimal n prevents confident interpretation, but the distribution (which shows scores spread across 2–9 with a gap at 6) is consistent with a bimodal response pattern. Qualitative data (n=16) shows a client/patient-heavy listener mix and change_rating clustering at 'Noticeable improvement' with no Significant improvements reported — possibly indicating the protocol is reaching its ceiling effect for this population without fully delivering the intended transformation.
X-MC-L3.sphere_plain: Cosmos is the one that warrants the most careful attention in the whole library right now. With only 10 outcome scores, we can't say much with confidence — but what we see is a very wide range of responses, some people benefiting strongly and others much less so. The qualitative data, while slightly larger, suggests the protocol is reaching a clinical population who find it helpful but not as powerfully as hoped. This one needs more data before we can understand what's happening.

sphere_commentary.overview: Across 1,000 outcome scores spanning 30 protocols, the corpus returns a weighted mean in the 6.3–6.8 range — a coherent signal of moderate-to-good subjective benefit that holds across all three series without any series falling below the meaningful threshold. Standard deviations cluster between 1.3 and 1.9, indicating healthy population-level variance rather than ceiling effects or systematic failure. At this sample size the aggregate picture is stable enough to shift analytical focus toward within-series and within-subcategory differentiation.
sphere_commentary.cross_series: HEALING leads marginally (mean 6.78, n=582) with the largest sample and most developed confidence profile, which is expected given its deployment priority and listener volume. THRIVING (mean 6.73, n=242) performs comparably despite a fundamentally different protocol type — HOLD rather than DESCENT — suggesting the phi-sigmoid architecture translates reasonably well to maintenance and creative states. TRANSFORMING trails (mean 6.36, n=176) with the smallest samples and highest proportion of developing-confidence protocols, which is structurally appropriate: ascending-arc protocols for low-affect populations are the hardest to design and the least likely to have found their full audience yet.
sphere_commentary.anomalies: Three patterns warrant flagging. First, X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32) shows the highest standard deviation in the corpus alongside the lowest mean in TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity subcategory — a spread wide enough to suggest either population mismatch or a bimodal response split between listeners who benefit strongly and those who do not; n is too small to distinguish these hypotheses but the signal is directionally unusual. Second, T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38) underperforms relative to its L1 and L2 siblings (6.40 and 7.09 respectively) — a non-monotonic level progression that breaks the expected deepening-benefit pattern and warrants structural review. Third, T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15) shows a notably irregular distribution with a near-empty score at position 6 flanked by a cluster at 7 and an isolated score at 10, which for n=15 may be noise but resembles an early bimodal pattern; the 1-minute data also flags 4 of 19 qualitative respondents reporting 'Did NOT settle', an unusually high proportion for a HOLD protocol.
sphere_commentary.development_signals: 1. T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23): The non-monotonic drop from T-FC-L2's 7.09 is the clearest within-series regression in the dataset. The 1-minute data shows settle_time heavily weighted to 'About 10 minutes' (14/26 respondents) and rhythm rated 'Slightly busy' by 6 respondents — consistent with a protocol that may be over-complex at the L3 position rather than providing deeper entrainment. Recommend reviewing the hold-phase density and wing-ratio calibration in Summit's architecture; consider whether the L3 HOLD format is introducing too much rhythmic complexity for listeners who arrived expecting deepened ease. Action: audit Summit's phi-sigmoid curve parameters, specifically the ease-in duration relative to L2, and trial a simplified variant. 2. X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, std=2.32): The combination of lowest TRANSFORMING mean and highest corpus std at minimal n makes this the protocol most urgently needing a population investigation. The 1-minute data (n=16) shows a client/patient-heavy listener mix (7/16) and change_rating skewed toward 'Noticeable improvement' with no 'Significant improvement' reported — suggesting the protocol is providing mild benefit to a clinical population that may need more aggressive descent scaffolding. Action: increase n deliberately by routing more TRANSFORMING listeners through Cosmos, and add a population-split question to the 1-minute survey to distinguish self-directed from clinician-observed responses before drawing structural conclusions. 3. H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14): This is the lowest-performing crisis protocol and the only one where music_opinion mode lands at 'Neutral / so-so / meh' rather than 'Liked it' — a rare divergence from the rest of the crisis subcategory. H-CR-L1 and L3 both receive warmer music reception and score higher. The 1-minute data also shows a bimodal settle_time with a secondary mode at 'About 20 minutes', suggesting Harbor may be too gradual for listeners in acute distress who need earlier anchoring. Action: review Harbor's opening descent phase and consider compressing the early wing-ratio to front-load regulation signal; also investigate whether the musical palette is mismatched to the L2 listener who has already engaged with Anchor and carries different expectations. 4. X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, pct_pos=36%): The lowest pct_pos in the ASCENT subcategory and one of the lowest in the corpus — only 36% of respondents scored above the midpoint. The 1-minute qualitative data shows settle_time bimodally split between 'About 20 minutes' and 'About 10 minutes', with 'Loved it' dominant in music_opinion but 'Slightly busy' appearing in rhythm — suggesting the musical appreciation is not translating into the functional uplift the ASCENT protocol is designed to produce. The rhythm being perceived as busy for a motivational protocol is a specific signal: the drive cues may be activating arousal without providing sufficient forward-momentum structure. Action: investigate whether Trail's ASCENT arc has sufficient progressive build in the mid-section, and consider whether the L2 entry point is positioned correctly between Footpath and Switchback in the motivational gradient.

by_protocol.H-AX-L1: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89, confidence=strong) is the highest-performing Anxiety & Stress protocol and among the strongest in the corpus, though its std of 1.89 indicates meaningful spread across the population. The distribution is right-weighted with peaks at scores 6–9, consistent with broad but not universal benefit. Qualitative data (n=23) shows a mixed listener population with notable caregiver and clinician presence (10/23 non-self reporters), change_rating dominated by Moderate and Noticeable improvement, and rhythm perceived as Balanced/comfortable by a strong majority — suggesting the protocol's entrainment rate is well-calibrated for its DESCENT function.
by_protocol.H-AX-L2: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.90, std=1.63, confidence=strong) shows tighter variance than L1 while maintaining comparable central tendency, which may reflect a more homogeneous responding population at L2. The distribution is reasonably symmetric around 7–8. Qualitative data (n=20) flags a bimodal settle_time split between 5 and 20 minutes — potentially reflecting two listener subgroups: those who entrain quickly and those requiring the full session. Driving/commuting appearing as a notable activity context (4/20) warrants attention given DESCENT protocol mechanics.
by_protocol.H-AX-L3: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81, confidence=meaningful) performs strongly at L3 and maintains the subcategory's broadly positive signal. The qualitative settle_time data is notable: 'Near the end — the entire session' appears as a secondary mode (4/20), suggesting some listeners require the full arc of a longer protocol to regulate — consistent with the WING RATIO architecture producing more gradual entrainment at L3. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling/calming' most frequently, the strongest positive rhythm rating in the AX subcategory.
by_protocol.H-CR-L1: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) performs above structural expectation for a crisis-entry protocol, where depressed baseline states typically compress outcome scores. pct_pos at 52% is low in absolute terms but appropriate given the population. Qualitative data (n=24) is notably positive: change_rating is unusually strong with 8/24 reporting Significant improvement — the highest in the crisis subcategory — and music reception is warm. The 'Other' activity category dominating (6/24) suggests unstructured, spontaneous use in distress moments, which is exactly the intended deployment context.
by_protocol.H-CR-L2: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67, confidence=meaningful) is the weakest performer in the crisis subcategory and notable for being the only crisis protocol where music_opinion mode is 'Neutral/so-so' rather than positive. Settle_time shows a secondary mode at 20 minutes, suggesting entrainment may be too gradual for listeners in acute distress who have already completed L1. Confidence is meaningful but warrants structural review before the sample grows further.
by_protocol.H-CR-L3: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85, confidence=meaningful) recovers slightly from L2, which is an unexpected non-monotonic pattern within the crisis arc. Qualitative settle_time is striking: 'Immediately/within the first minute or two' is the modal response (7/19), suggesting Horizon may be working for listeners who are primed from earlier protocols and respond rapidly to a familiar sonic environment. However, 4/19 music_opinion responses are 'Not liked' — the highest negative music rating in the HEALING series — warranting a review of the L3 sonic palette.
by_protocol.H-OS-L1: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80, confidence=strong) is the largest-sample protocol in the corpus and returns a moderate positive signal with notable spread. The distribution shows a concentration at score 6 (19 respondents) with reasonable tails in both directions, suggesting a population split between strong responders and those experiencing minimal change. Qualitative data (n=27) shows a diverse listener mix and Driving/commuting as a top activity context (5/27) — relevant for an over-stimulation protocol, where environmental noise may interfere with entrainment.
by_protocol.H-OS-L2: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85, confidence=strong) shows improvement over L1 in mean score, consistent with expected level progression. The settle_time data clusters at 10 minutes (10/20 qualitative respondents), suggesting Rainfall's entrainment arc is well-matched to listener expectation at this depth. Rhythm perception is evenly split between 'Very settling/calming' and 'Balanced/comfortable', both positive categories, indicating no friction with the DESCENT structure.
by_protocol.H-OS-L3: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78, confidence=meaningful) reaches the highest mean in the OS subcategory and shows strong rhythm reception — a perfect split between 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Very settling/calming' (10 each, n=23 qualitative). Activity context is dominated by 'Trying to sleep' (8/23), which is off-label for an over-stimulation protocol but suggests listeners are self-routing Glacier as a sleep aid — a potential cross-series routing signal worth investigating.
by_protocol.H-SL-L1: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89, confidence=strong) performs well with a broad distribution spanning scores 5–9. Qualitative data (n=15) is unexpectedly light relative to the outcome n, limiting contextual inference, but shows warm music reception (10/15 Liked, 3/15 Loved) and fast settle_time for many respondents. The Therapy session activity appearing (3/15) alongside Studying is an unusual context for a sleep-oriented protocol — may reflect clinicians using it for general relaxation induction.
by_protocol.H-SL-L2: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46, confidence=meaningful) has the tightest standard deviation in the HEALING series, suggesting a more homogeneous response profile than its siblings — a possible indicator that Hammock is reaching a well-matched population. However, qualitative data flags concerns: listener_type is dominated by clients/patients and support-persons rather than self-listeners (11/15 non-self), music_opinion mode is 'Neutral/so-so', and rhythm receives a 'Too busy' rating from 3/15 respondents — the only appearance of that category in the sleep subcategory. This warrants investigation given the low-variability outcome score may be masking population-specific mismatches.
by_protocol.H-SL-L3: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57, confidence=meaningful) maintains strong performance at L3 with consistent change_rating distribution in qualitative data. Notably, 7/22 qualitative respondents report 'Slightly busy' rhythm — an elevated rate for a sleep-descent protocol at L3, where maximum settling would be expected. This may reflect the phi-sigmoid architecture's longer wing ratios producing perceptible rhythmic movement even at low tempos, and warrants a review of Midnight's L3 complexity level.
by_protocol.T-CF-L1: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63, confidence=meaningful) returns a moderate positive signal for a Creative Flow entry protocol. The qualitative settle_time is bimodal — 'Immediately' and 'About 10 minutes' are tied at 6 respondents each — which may reflect two listener populations: those using Ember for flow-state maintenance versus those arriving in a less-optimal state and requiring longer entrainment. Change_rating distribution is spread across Slight, Moderate, and Significant improvement, suggesting Ember is providing differentiated benefit across listener states.
by_protocol.T-CF-L2: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28, confidence=meaningful) shows the tightest variance in the Creative Flow subcategory and the strongest music reception — mode 'Loved it' tied with 'Liked it' (6 each, n=15 qualitative). The narrow std suggests Current is reaching a consistent subpopulation, possibly committed creative practitioners who self-select into L2. The relatively modest mean given the strong music appreciation warrants investigation: high aesthetic response without proportionally high outcome score may suggest the protocol is engaging but not fully optimising the intended HOLD function.
by_protocol.T-CF-L3: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15, confidence=developing) is the highest-mean Creative Flow protocol and has the tightest variance in the subcategory, though n=18 limits confidence. The qualitative data (n=25 — notably larger than outcome n, suggesting response timing asymmetry) shows strong representation from caregivers and clinicians (13/25 non-self), which may be inflating or deflating the 1-minute responses relative to actual listeners. Rhythm is overwhelmingly 'Balanced/comfortable' (14/25), consistent with a well-calibrated HOLD architecture at depth.
by_protocol.T-FC-L1: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.40, std=1.77, confidence=meaningful) shows the lowest mean in the Focus & Clarity subcategory despite having the largest sample. The qualitative data (n=23) is revealing: rhythm_opinion mode is 'Slightly busy' (9/23) — the strongest busy signal in the THRIVING series — while music is warmly received (15/23 Liked). This dissociation between music appreciation and rhythm perception suggests the HOLD architecture may be introducing too much textural movement for a focus-entry protocol, where listeners need clean cognitive scaffolding rather than rhythmic engagement.
by_protocol.T-FC-L2: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51, confidence=meaningful) is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity subcategory and shows a well-distributed outcome score with a peak at score 8. The settle_time mode at 'About 10 minutes' (9/17 qualitative) is consistent with the longer entrainment curve expected at L2. The 'Slightly busy' rhythm rating from 5/17 respondents is lower than L1's rate but still present, suggesting a thread of complexity that persists across the FC subcategory.
by_protocol.T-FC-L3: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38, confidence=meaningful) is the primary anomaly in the THRIVING series: a clear underperformance relative to L2 in a subcategory where the protocol should be deepening benefit. Qualitative change_rating is paradoxically the strongest in the FC subcategory (11/26 report Significant improvement), creating a disconnect between 1-second outcome scores and 1-minute change perception that may reflect different response windows or population self-selection at L3. Settle_time at 10 minutes is the dominant mode (14/26), and rhythm 'Slightly busy' persists (6/26), consistent with a protocol that demands more from the listener than it returns.
by_protocol.T-MB-L1: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31, confidence=meaningful) is the strongest performer in THRIVING and ties with T-CF-L3 for the highest THRIVING mean. Qualitative data shows heavy therapy session use (8/23) and a bimodal settle_time between 'About 20 minutes' and 'About 5 minutes' — an unusually wide spread suggesting Stone is used both as a session anchor and a quick-reset tool. Music reception is mixed but neutral-to-positive, which for a HOLD protocol is functionally appropriate.
by_protocol.T-MB-L2: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) has the highest standard deviation in the THRIVING series, indicating wider population variance than its siblings. Qualitative rhythm data shows a perfect split between 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Slightly busy' (7 each, n=16), suggesting the protocol sits at a perceptual boundary for complexity tolerance. The client/patient and self-listener split is nearly equal (7:7), which may be contributing to the variance.
by_protocol.T-MB-L3: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64, confidence=developing) has a structurally unusual score distribution — a dense cluster at score 7 (9 respondents) with sparse values elsewhere and an isolated score at 10, producing a pattern that warrants monitoring for bimodality as n grows. The qualitative 'Did NOT settle' rate of 4/19 is the most concerning signal in the THRIVING series; for a L3 HOLD protocol aimed at balance maintenance, non-settlement may indicate a population routing issue rather than a protocol flaw.
by_protocol.X-MC-L1: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56, confidence=meaningful) is the strongest-performing TRANSFORMING protocol in this subcategory and shows the warmest music reception in the series — 'Loved it' and 'Liked it' tied at 9 each (n=25 qualitative). Rhythm is predominantly positive (Balanced/comfortable and Very settling/calming totalling 17/25). Despite 2/25 qualitative respondents reporting 'It actually made things worse', the aggregate signal is constructive. The worse-experience responses warrant a follow-up question addition to understand whether these reflect pre-existing state severity or protocol mismatch.
by_protocol.X-MC-L2: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37, confidence=developing) shows a strongly self-listener-dominated population in qualitative data (16/20 reporting for themselves), which is distinctive in the TRANSFORMING series where caregiver/clinician use is more common. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling/calming' by 12/20 respondents — the strongest calming rhythm signal in the entire TRANSFORMING series. Settle_time is bimodal (immediate vs. 10 minutes), potentially reflecting two listener entry states.
by_protocol.X-MC-L3: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.60, std=2.32, confidence=developing) is the highest-variance, lowest-mean protocol in the TRANSFORMING series and the clearest anomaly in the corpus. The wide std at minimal n prevents confident interpretation, but the distribution (which shows scores spread across 2–9 with a gap at 6) is consistent with a bimodal response pattern. Qualitative data (n=16) shows a client/patient-heavy listener mix and change_rating clustering at 'Noticeable improvement' with no Significant improvements reported — possibly indicating the protocol is reaching its ceiling effect for this population without fully delivering the intended transformation.
by_protocol.X-MD-L1: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) is the entry point for TRANSFORMING's Motivation & Drive ASCENT subcategory and shows a broad, relatively flat distribution across scores 4–9. Qualitative settle_time is fast (modal response: About 5 minutes, with Immediately as secondary), which is consistent with an ASCENT protocol that begins energising listeners relatively quickly. Music reception is positive-to-neutral and rhythm is warm, suggesting the sonic design is not creating friction even if the motivational outcome is moderate.
by_protocol.X-MD-L2: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.00, std=1.57, confidence=developing) returns the lowest pct_pos (36%) in the Motivation & Drive subcategory and one of the lowest in the corpus. Qualitative data shows 'Loved it' as music_opinion mode (9/21) — a striking dissociation between aesthetic appreciation and functional outcome. Rhythm is perceived as 'Slightly busy' by 6/21 respondents, which for an ASCENT protocol may indicate the drive cues are activating arousal without productive forward-momentum structure. The settle_time skewing late (20 minutes modal) suggests Trail's build phase may peak too slowly for a mid-level motivational protocol.
by_protocol.X-MD-L3: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, confidence=developing) shows the highest mean in the Motivation & Drive subcategory despite the highest standard deviation — a pattern consistent with a protocol that works very well for some listeners and not at all for others. Qualitative change_rating is predominantly Moderate improvement (8/17) with fast settle_time (modal: About 5 minutes), and the activity context is dominated by 'Trying to sleep' (6/17) — an off-label use for an ASCENT protocol that may be artificially depressing outcome scores if listeners are using Switchback as a wind-down tool.
by_protocol.X-ME-L1: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) is the entry point for the Mood & Energy ASCENT subcategory with a moderate positive signal and relatively flat distribution between scores 4–8. Qualitative data shows strong caregiver and clinician presence (11/22 non-self) and settle_time skewing late (modal: About 20 minutes, 6/22). 'Loved it' music_opinion (10/22) is strong, but 'Neutral' also appears prominently (6/22), with rhythm predominantly Balanced — suggesting the ASCENT arc is functioning smoothly but may need a stronger energising signal in the early phase given the long settle times.
by_protocol.X-ME-L2: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64, confidence=meaningful) improves over L1 in mean and pct_pos (60% vs 43%), which is the expected level progression for an ASCENT subcategory. Qualitative change_rating is strongly weighted toward Significant improvement (7/15), the most positive distribution in the Mood & Energy subcategory. Settle_time is bimodal — 'About 20 minutes' and 'Immediately' tied — which may reflect a similar two-population dynamic as seen in other TRANSFORMING protocols.
by_protocol.X-ME-L3: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74, confidence=developing) underperforms relative to L2, creating a non-monotonic progression in the Mood & Energy subcategory (6.29 → 6.48 → 6.28). Qualitative change_rating shows 5/25 respondents reporting 'No effect' — the highest no-effect rate in the TRANSFORMING series — alongside 6 reporting Significant improvement, suggesting a polarised response. Activity context dominated by Relaxing and Doing chores (10/25 combined) may indicate Updraft is not being used in optimal conditions for an ascending-arc protocol.

by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L1: Meadow is one of the better-performing protocols in the whole library — most people who use it report feeling noticeably calmer afterward, and the results are consistent enough to take seriously with 65 responses. There's still quite a range in how much it helps different people, which is normal. Caregivers and therapists are using it alongside individuals, and most describe it as balanced and comfortable to listen to — not too much, not too little.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L2: Cottage continues the calming arc from Meadow and holds up well as a second step. Most people report real improvement, and the tighter spread of results suggests it's reaching a fairly consistent group of listeners. One thing worth noting: a good number of people seem to be listening while driving, which is a bit unexpected for a protocol designed to help you slow down — something to keep an eye on.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L3: Valley, the deepest anxiety protocol, performs just as well as the earlier levels — which is reassuring. Some people need almost the whole session before they start to feel the shift, which makes sense for a longer, more gradual experience. Listeners describe the rhythm as especially calming at this level, more so than in Meadow or Cottage, suggesting the deeper architecture is doing its job.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L1: Anchor is designed for moments of real distress, so the results need to be read carefully — people arrive in difficult states, and even getting to a 6 out of 10 from there is meaningful. What stands out in the qualitative responses is that a surprising number of people report significant improvement, more than you'd expect for a crisis protocol. It's clearly being used spontaneously in hard moments, which is exactly what it's there for.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L2: Harbor is the softest performer in the crisis group, and the qualitative responses add some texture to why: the music gets a more lukewarm reception than Anchor or Horizon, and some people take a long time to settle — up to 20 minutes. For people in acute distress, a slow start may not be ideal. This one warrants a closer look at whether the opening section is doing enough to create early steadiness.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L3: Horizon, the third crisis protocol, recovers a little from Harbor's dip, which is an interesting pattern. Many people report settling very quickly — sometimes within the first couple of minutes — possibly because they've already been through the earlier protocols and the sound feels familiar. A small but notable group didn't enjoy the music, which is worth paying attention to given that the other crisis protocols receive warmer responses.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L1: Pebble has the most responses of any protocol in the library, which gives us real confidence in what we're seeing. Results are positive on average but vary quite a bit between individuals — some people feel a strong shift, others less so. A number of listeners report using it while driving, which is an interesting real-world use case for a protocol designed to help with overstimulation.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L2: Rainfall shows improvement over Pebble, with most qualitative respondents settling around the 10-minute mark — a sign the pacing feels right. People describe the rhythm warmly, and there's no notable friction. A solid, reliable second step in the overstimulation series.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L3: Glacier is the strongest overstimulation protocol and receives consistently warm feedback on both music and rhythm. Interestingly, a lot of people seem to be using it to help them sleep — even though it's technically designed for overstimulation. That overlap is worth exploring: Glacier may be quietly doing double duty as a sleep aid for some listeners.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L1: Lantern performs well as a sleep entry point, with a mostly positive response and listeners describing it as easy and pleasant. The sample for the qualitative feedback is smaller than expected relative to how many people completed the quick survey, which limits what we can say about how people are actually experiencing it. What we do have suggests it's being used in therapy contexts as well as at home.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L2: Hammock has the most consistent results in the sleep series — people's responses cluster more tightly around a similar score than in the other sleep protocols, which suggests it's reaching a reliable group. However, the qualitative picture is more mixed: a few people find it a bit too rhythmically busy for a sleep protocol, and the music gets a cooler reception than Lantern. Worth investigating whether the pacing needs to ease off a little.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L3: Midnight performs strongly as the deepest sleep protocol, but a notable proportion of qualitative respondents describe the rhythm as 'slightly busy' — which feels counterintuitive for something designed to help you drift off. This may be an inherent feature of how longer protocols are structured, but it's worth exploring whether the final phase could be quieter and simpler.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L1: Ember gets creative flow started with moderate success — people report improvement, though often in the slight-to-moderate range rather than dramatic. There seem to be two different kinds of listeners using it: some settle almost immediately, others take closer to ten minutes. That split might reflect people using it in very different states when they press play.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L2: Current is notable for how much people enjoy the music — it's one of the most loved-sounding protocols in the library. The results are consistent and positive, though the mean score doesn't quite match the enthusiasm for the sound. People seem to really enjoy the listening experience, but the functional flow benefit may not be maximised yet — an interesting design question for the future.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L3: Aurora has the fewest responses of the Creative Flow protocols, so we're holding these findings lightly. What we do see is encouraging — the scores are the highest in the series and the results are quite consistent. Rhythm is described as balanced and comfortable by most. It's worth building the sample before drawing firm conclusions.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L1: Compass, the entry point for Focus & Clarity, is actually the lowest-scoring protocol in its subcategory — and the qualitative data hints at why. A lot of people describe the rhythm as 'slightly busy', which for a protocol meant to help you concentrate is a meaningful friction point. The music itself is liked, so this seems to be about the texture and pace rather than the sound quality.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L2: Waypoint is the best-performing Focus & Clarity protocol and shows the kind of improvement over L1 you'd hope to see. People settle around the 10-minute mark, which feels right for this kind of deeper focus work. A thread of 'slightly busy' rhythm perception persists, but it's less prominent than at L1 — suggesting the architecture is moving in the right direction.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L3: Summit is the most surprising result in the THRIVING series: it actually scores lower than Waypoint, when you'd expect the deepest protocol to go further. Paradoxically, people's own descriptions of their improvement are the most positive in the Focus & Clarity group — they feel like it helped — but the immediate outcome scores don't reflect that. This disconnect is worth investigating. The rhythm is still perceived as busy, and people take longer to settle at this level than might be ideal.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L1: Stone is the standout in the THRIVING series — the highest-scoring Maintenance & Balance protocol and one of the top performers overall. It's being used heavily in therapy settings, which may partly explain the strong results. People settle across a wide range of times, suggesting it's flexible enough to work in different contexts and session lengths.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L2: Axis shows more variability in outcomes than its siblings — people's experiences range more widely, which may reflect a more diverse audience at this level. The rhythm sits right at the edge of comfortable for some listeners, with a noticeable group finding it slightly busy. Results are still positive overall, but this is one to watch as more data comes in.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L3: Orbit is the deepest Maintenance & Balance protocol and the data is still early. What stands out is that 4 out of 19 qualitative respondents report they didn't settle at all — that's a higher-than-expected rate for a protocol at this level. It may mean some people are arriving here who aren't quite ready for L3, or it could be a routing issue. Worth investigating before the sample grows further.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L1: Cavern, the entry point for TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity protocols, is one of the most warmly received in the whole series — people genuinely seem to love the music and describe the rhythm as calming. Results are positive, though a small number of people reported it made things worse rather than better, which is worth tracking carefully as something to understand rather than dismiss.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L2: Starlight is striking for two reasons: it's used almost entirely by people listening for themselves (rather than caregivers or clinicians), and the rhythm is described as very settling by most — the most calming rhythm response in the whole TRANSFORMING series. Results are positive and consistent. The self-listener focus suggests this protocol may be attracting people who are doing serious, self-directed work on their mental state.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L3: Cosmos is the one that warrants the most careful attention in the whole library right now. With only 10 outcome scores, we can't say much with confidence — but what we see is a very wide range of responses, some people benefiting strongly and others much less so. The qualitative data, while slightly larger, suggests the protocol is reaching a clinical population who find it helpful but not as powerfully as hoped. This one needs more data before we can understand what's happening.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L1: Footpath, the entry into Motivation & Drive territory, provides moderate uplift and people settle relatively quickly — which is a good sign for an energising protocol. Results are spread across a wide range, reflecting the real diversity of people who arrive needing a motivational boost. The sonic experience itself lands positively, which gives a good foundation to build on.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L2: Trail is one of the most puzzling findings in the dataset. People love the music — it receives some of the warmest music responses in the TRANSFORMING series — but the functional outcomes are among the lowest in the corpus. Something about the listening experience is genuinely enjoyable but may not be translating into the energy and motivation lift it's designed to provide. The rhythm being described as slightly busy is one possible clue: drive cues that activate without directing may create arousal without momentum.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L3: Switchback has the widest spread of results in the Motivation & Drive protocols — some people respond really well, others much less so. Interestingly, a lot of people seem to be using it to try to sleep, even though it's an energising protocol. If that's happening regularly, those listeners are likely to score it lower simply because it's not doing what they need — which could be pulling the overall numbers down.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L1: Breeze opens the Mood & Energy series with a modest positive signal. People take a while to settle — often around 20 minutes — which may mean the early section of the protocol could do more to signal the shift that's coming. The music is warmly received by many, and caregivers and clinicians are a significant part of the audience, suggesting it's being used in supported contexts.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L2: Sunrise is the strongest performer in the Mood & Energy series and shows a meaningful step up from Breeze. The qualitative descriptions of improvement are notably positive — more people report significant improvement here than at any other level in the subcategory. The bimodal settle time (some people feeling it immediately, others taking much longer) suggests a mixed audience that responds to the protocol in quite different ways.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L3: Updraft, the deepest Mood & Energy protocol, dips back below Sunrise — a pattern worth watching. A meaningful number of people report no effect at all, which is the highest no-effect rate in the TRANSFORMING series. At the same time, others report significant improvement, suggesting the protocol may be working well for a specific subset while missing another. People often seem to be using it in low-demand contexts like relaxing or doing chores, which may not be the ideal environment for an ascending-arc protocol meant to lift mood and energy.
